verses.text: As they approached Jerusalem and came to Bethphage at the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two disciples,
He told them, “Go into the village ahead of you, and right there you will find a donkey tied, and a colt with her. Untie them and bring them to me.
If anyone asks you, ‘Why are you doing this?’ say, ‘The Lord needs them,’ and he will send them right away.”
This happened to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet:
“Say to the daughter of Zion: ‘Look, your King is coming to you, humble and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.’”
So the disciples went and did as Jesus instructed them.
They brought the donkey and the colt, placed their cloaks on them, and Jesus sat on them.
A large crowd spread their cloaks on the road, while others cut branches from the trees and spread them on the path.
The crowds that went ahead and those that followed shouted: “Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest!”
When Jesus entered Jerusalem, the whole city was stirred and asked, “Who is this?”
The crowds replied, “This is Jesus, the prophet from Nazareth in Galilee.”
Jesus entered the temple and drove out all who were buying and selling. He overturned the tables of the money changers and the benches of those selling doves.
He said to them, “It is written, ‘My house will be called a house of prayer,’ but you have made it a den of thieves.”
Then the blind and the lame came to him at the temple, and he healed them.
But when the chief priests and scribes saw the wonderful things he did, and the children shouting in the temple, “Hosanna to the Son of David!”, they were indignant
They said to him, “Do you hear what these children are saying?” Jesus replied, “Yes. Have you never read, ‘Out of the mouths of children and infants you have perfected praise’?”
Then he left them and went out of the city to Bethany, where he spent the night.
In the morning, as he returned to the city, he became hungry.
Seeing a fig tree by the road, he went to it but found nothing except leaves. He said to it, “May you never bear fruit ever again.” And at once, the fig tree withered.
When the disciples saw this, they were astonished and said, “How did the fig tree wither so quickly?”

Jesus answered, “Truly, I tell you, if you have faith and do not doubt, not only will you do what was done to the fig tree, but even if you say to this mountain, ‘Be lifted up and thrown into the sea,’ it will happen.
And anything you ask in prayer, believing, you will receive.”
When Jesus entered the temple, the chief priests and the elders confronted him as he was teaching and said, “By what authority are you doing these things, and who gave you this authority?”
Jesus replied, “I will also ask you one question, and if you answer me, I will tell you by what authority I do these things.
The baptism of John; where was it from? From heaven or from people?” They discussed this among themselves, saying, “If we say, ‘From heaven,’ he will ask, ‘Then why did you not believe him?’
But if we say, ‘From people,’ we fear the crowd, for they all regard John as a prophet.”
So they answered Jesus, “We do not know.” Then he said, “Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.”

sections.headline: The Parable of the Two Sons

verses.text: “What do you think? A man had two sons. He went to the first and said, ‘Son, go and work today in my vineyard.’
The son answered, ‘I will not,’ but later he regretted it and went.
Then the man went to the second son and said the same. He answered, ‘I will, sir,’ but he did not go.
Which of the two did the will of his father?” They replied, “The first.” Jesus said, “Truly, I tell you, tax collectors and prostitutes are entering the kingdom of God ahead of you.
For John came to you in the way of righteousness, but you did not believe him. Yet tax collectors and prostitutes believed him, and even after you saw this, you did not repent and believe him.”

sections.headline: The Parable of the Wicked Tenants

verses.text: Listen to another parable: There was a landowner who planted a vineyard, put a hedge around it, dug a winepress, and built a tower. Then he leased it to tenants and went away.
When the harvest approached, he sent his servants to the tenants to collect his fruit.
But the tenants seized his servants. They beat one, killed another, and stoned a third.
Again, he sent more servants than the first, and they treated them the same way.
Then last of all he sent his son to them, saying, ‘They will respect my son.’
But when the tenants saw the son, they said to each other, ‘This is the heir. Come, let us kill him and take his inheritance.’
So they seized him, threw him out of the vineyard, and killed him.
So when the owner of the vineyard comes, what will he do to those tenants?
They replied, “He will destroy those wicked tenants in a terrible way and lease the vineyard to others who will give him the fruits in their season.”
Jesus said to them, “Have you never read in the Scriptures: ‘The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. This was the Lord's doing, and it is marvelous in our eyes.’
Therefore, I tell you, the kingdom of God will be taken from you and given to a people who will produce its fruit.
And whoever falls on this stone will be broken, but on whomever it falls, it will crush them to pieces.
When the chief priests and Pharisees heard his parables, they realized he was speaking about them.
But when they sought to arrest him, they feared the crowds, because they considered him to be a prophet.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “the foal of a donkey” is a reference to a young, unbroken donkey, emphasizing the humility and peace of the Messiah's approach, in contrast to the war horses typically ridden by conquering kings. This echoes the prophecy of Zechariah 9:9, symbolizing a peaceful, righteous ruler rather than a military conqueror.

The phrase “you have perfected praise” is a direct quotation from Psalm 8:2 in the Greek Septuagint, where the word katartizō (perfected) carries the sense of establishing, completing, or making fully ready. It emphasizes the idea that even the simplest, most unpolished voices can offer pure and complete praise, reflecting God's ability to bring divine purpose to full expression through humble vessels.

The phrase “the stone the builders rejected” is a direct reference to Psalm 118:22-23, which originally described the surprising victory of a seemingly insignificant stone becoming the most critical part of the structure. In the context of this parable, Jesus identifies himself as the cornerstone, rejected by the religious leaders but chosen by God to be the foundation of the new covenant. The term “marvelous” captures both the unexpected nature and the divine significance of this reversal, aligning with the broader biblical theme of God's wisdom confounding human expectations.
